name: Pishacha
description: Pishachas are malevolent flesh-eating demons prominent in Hindu mythology. They are often depicted as grotesque and terrifying beings, embodying impurity, decay, and the darker aspects of existence. These demons are believed to haunt cremation grounds, desolate places, and areas associated with death and disease. Pishachas are said to feast on human flesh, particularly corpses, and can possess human beings, driving them to madness or causing illness. They are shape-shifters, capable of assuming various forms, often appearing as animals or distorted human figures to deceive and terrify their victims. Their presence is considered extremely inauspicious, and rituals are often performed to ward them off. Pishachas are associated with the night and darkness, representing the antithesis of purity and righteousness. They are considered servants of the goddess Nirriti, the deity of misfortune and destruction.
subtitle: Malevolent flesh-eating demons of Hindu mythology
appearance: Grotesque, often with bulging eyes, sharp teeth, and emaciated bodies. They can shapeshift but often appear as distorted humans or animals.
habitat: Cremation grounds, desolate places, areas associated with death and disease, the night.
behavior: Malicious and predatory. They feast on corpses, spread disease, and delight in causing fear and chaos. They are known to possess humans, driving them mad.

keyMyths.title: Harishchandra's Trial
keyMyths.description: King Harishchandra, known for his truthfulness, was tested by the sage Vishwamitra. He was forced to work at a cremation ground, where Pishachas tormented him, trying to break his resolve and make him abandon his dharma. However, Harishchandra remained steadfast, proving his unwavering commitment to truth. This story highlights the Pishachas' association with cremation grounds and their role as tormentors.

keyMyths.title: Vikram and the Vampire (Vetala)
keyMyths.description: While not strictly Pishachas, Vetala, often considered a type of Pishacha, feature prominently in the Vetala Panchavimshati stories. King Vikramaditya is tasked with capturing a Vetala, a corpse possessed by a spirit. Each time Vikram tries to take the Vetala to the tantric, the Vetala tells a story that ends with a riddle. When Vikram solves the riddle, the Vetala escapes, highlighting the cunning and deceptive nature of these creatures.

keyMyths.title: Pishacha Possession
keyMyths.description: Various folk tales describe instances of Pishachas possessing individuals, causing physical and mental ailments. Exorcisms and rituals are performed to drive the Pishacha out of the possessed person. These stories emphasize the fear and danger associated with these demons and the importance of spiritual protection.
